⍞ Materials: Transformed By Truth, notebook

➜ INTRO
How would you answer someone who asks why you study the Bible? As the title indicates, the first half of Transformed by Truth will focus on the why of studying the Bible, while the second half will move into the how of Bible study. As we move through the book's second half, we will apply the lessons to a chapter from your reading in the book of Acts.

➜ READ & NARRATE
Intro p.13-16

➜ NOTEBOOK
Take a moment to reflect on the author's statement, "Teens need serious Bible study just as much as adults." Do you agree? Why or why not? What do you hope to learn about Bible study this year?

➜ INTRO
Part of Bible study starts with basic questions, such as, "What does the Bible say about this matter?" Rather than taking an author's opinion or statement as truth when claiming the Bible supports it, verifying and reading what the Bible says on the subject is important. As you read through Transformed by Truth, look up the Scripture references that are included as time allows. Today, those verses are assigned as part of the reading.

➜ READ, NARRATE & DISCUSS
p.31-36 "When I was" - "others. Praise God!"
Also read the verses in the passage: Jer. 1:1-2; Ezek. 1:3; 2 Pet. 1:20-21; 2 Pet. 3:15-16; 2 Tim. 3:16; I Cor. 7:40

• Did reading the verses from the source (the Bible) help clarify or support the author's claim?

➜ NOTEBOOK
Take a moment to record new insights, copy quotes, or ask questions.

⍞ Materials: Linked article

➜ RECAP
What did you read about last time?

➜ INTRO
As the author has discussed, part of studying the Bible is understanding the "Big Story" of the Bible and its metanarrative. What does it mean for the Bible to be read as literature? Instead of God giving us a bullet-point list of attributes about who he is and what he is like, he shows us his character through the stories of how he interacted with people. Just as fictional stories use different elements such as characters, setting, plot, climax, and resolution to tell a good story, the Bible uses the same elements to tell the true stories of God's people. When we understand these elements better, we can understand the Bible better. "A book needs to be read in keeping with its author's intention. We can see from the Bible itself that it is a thoroughly literary book. God superintended its authors to write a very (though not wholly) literary book. Paying adequate attention to the literary qualities of the Bible not only helps to unlock the meanings of the Bible; it is also a way of honoring the literary intentions of its authors. Surely, biblical authors regarded everything they put into their writing as important. We also need to regard those things as important." (How Bible Stories Work, p.10) This quote is from Leland Ryken, a retired professor and author. Today's reading is an article Ryken wrote about reading the Bible as literature.

➜ READ & NARRATE
∞ Article Link: Ten Things You Should Know About the Bible as Literature

➜ NOTEBOOK
Add any notes, thoughts, or questions about the article in your notebook.

Exam Week

➜ EXAMS
Answer question(s) related to course.

Questions will come from:
Transformed by Truth
assignment_teachersNote:
★ Spiritual Formation: Grades 7-8 — TERM 1 EXAM QUESTIONS
· Transformed by Truth: Explain how the Bible is a "Big Story." What elements of story do we see in the Bible? Give examples of those elements from the Bible story.

· Transformed by Truth: Give two of the reasons the Bible should be studied, along with explanations of those reasons.

Using at least two different resources, look up and read an introduction to Acts to find information about the Historical, Biblical, and Cultural context of Acts. The Ultimate Bible Atlas, The Chronological Guide to the Bible, and the ESV Concise Study Bible you use in your Bible reading all contain contextual background information. Alternatively, you could use an online site such as Bible Project or Blue Letter Bible, or ask a parent what Bible commentaries or Bible dictionaries they may have that could be used.
∞ Link: Blue Letter Bible (click on Tools for commentaries)
∞ Link: The Bible Project

➜ NOTEBOOK

➜ NOTEBOOK
Using your Acts 17 printout, do the following:
• Read through Acts 17 looking for repeated words, themes, or phrases. Some examples are given below, but if you find any others on your own, mark those.
• Mark all words related to thinking, studying, and reasoning (synonyms or antonyms) in red.
• Mark all cities Paul travels to in green.
• Mark all names of people mentioned in brown.
• Mark any reactions of people listening to the message Paul is preaching in blue. Is there a difference in reactions from different groups or individuals?
• If you find any other themes you want to mark, do so now. Ideas might include action words (persuaded, joined, attacked, etc.), responses from women, or words referring to or having to do with God (Lord, word of God, etc.).
• What is the main issue that is making the people angry?

2\. Keeping in mind the main idea of Acts, which shows the growth of the early church, how does Acts 17 fit into the flow of the book? How does what Luke includes in his synopsis of events help support his main purpose in writing Acts?

2\. Look up the following cross-references for "philosophers" and write down any observations you may have: 1 Corinthians 1:20-21; Romans 1:22; Colossians 2:6-8.
3\. Pick another verse or passage from Acts 17 that you would like to study more closely. Use a Bible Dictionary, or footnotes in a Study Bible, to research the passage's meaning. (Blueletterbible.org links to study notes or dictionaries if you cannot access one.) Write observations in your notebook.
∞ Link: Blue Letter Bible
4\. Use either the online Blue Letter Bible or a Strong's Concordance to look up the Strong's number, Greek word, and definition for the word "babbler" found in Acts 17:18.
5\. Using a commentary from your home, church, or library, look up the passage and write down any notes about what you learn about the passage. (Blueletterbible.org also has links to commentaries.)

⍞ Materials: This Changes Everything, notebook

➜ INTRO
In this lesson, we will begin the book This Changes Everything: How the Gospel Transforms the Teen Years. The author states in her intro that this book is for young people who want to grow in their spiritual life. "...Because we love Jesus. If we love Jesus, we'll love truth, and we'll want to grow" (p.14). And - as the title states - the author shows how the gospel changes our entire life and touches every area of our existence.

➜ READ & NARRATE
Ch.1 p.17-29

➜ NOTEBOOK
Choose two of the questions on p.29 to answer in your notebook.

⍞ Materials: This Changes Everything, notebook

➜ RECAP

➜ READ & NARRATE
Ch.2 p.31-42

• Toward the beginning of the year, lessons 5 and 6 covered the chapter in Transformed by Truth that focused on the Story of the Bible. Today's reading also covered the subject. Why is it good to be reminded of the overarching story of the Bible? Are you able to see yourself as part of that story? Why or why not? Is there any part of this subject you think the authors missed in their chapters?

➜ NOTEBOOK
Choose two of the questions from p.42-43 and answer them in your notebook.

⍞ Materials: This Changes Everything, notebook

➜ INTRO
Would you consider yourself an introvert or an extrovert? Maybe the word "community" is a bit intimidating! Today's chapter is about how the gospel affects our relationship with the people in our church. And thankfully, there's space for both introverts and extroverts!

➜ READ & NARRATE
p.45-56 "The gospel is" - "change anybody's life."

➜ NOTEBOOK

Write out any other responses or thoughts about the author's points about community.

• EXTRA HELPING
View the video at the link where Bible teacher Mike Winger goes through Eight Reasons God Wants You to Go to Church. Write the reasons and excuses he gives and then write a short response in your notebook.
